metadata.summary: Research-based fictional characters representing key user types that distill observed goals, behaviors, frustrations, and usage context into shared team artifacts for design decisions.

content.title: Personas
content.summary: Personas are research-based fictional characters representing key user types. Each persona distills observed goals, behaviors, frustrations, and usage context from real data into a shared team artifact that makes design decisions answerable: "Would Sarah want this?" Without them, teams design for themselves.

why.p: Teams without shared user models debate features as opinion battles with no resolution mechanism. Personas give every design and prioritization discussion a concrete reference: a named character with documented goals and pain points sourced from research. The artifact only carries signal when grounded in data — proto-personas and research-based personas serve different phases, but both must be explicit about their evidence basis.

list.item: Synthesizing interview, survey, analytics, and support-ticket data into 3-5 representative user types.
Aligning a team that disagrees about who the primary user is.
Bootstrapping proto-personas before research with explicit assumptions to validate.
Re-validating outdated personas after a product pivot or new market entry.
Writing user stories, A/B hypotheses, and onboarding flows that reference a consistent user identity.
Marketing-only segmentation (psychographics, ad targeting) — those need separate marketing-persona models.
Single-user products (internal tools with one stakeholder) where a persona adds zero signal.
Teams with no plan to reference personas in design reviews — building them as posters wastes effort.
High-stakes regulated domains (medical devices, aerospace) where formal user-task analysis supersedes lightweight personas.

section.name: persona-types

rule.statement: Choose persona type based on research maturity: research-based when data exists, proto-personas before data collection, provisional when assumptions must be made explicit.
rule.rationale: Mixing types without labeling them leads teams to treat assumptions as validated findings. Each type has a different confidence level and different update trigger.

section.p: Type comparison: research-based (data from interviews, surveys, analytics — most reliable); provisional (assumptions to validate — explicit uncertainty); proto-personas (team's current understanding — quick alignment before research).

rule.statement: Every persona must include: name, demographics (context), goals (what they want to achieve), frustrations (pain points), behaviors (how they act), a representative quote sourced from real data, and usage context (when/where/devices).
rule.rationale: Demographics-only personas are useless for design decisions. Goals and behaviors are the decision-making levers; demographics are context only.

rule.statement: Limit primary personas to 3-5. Each must represent a distinct combination of goals, behaviors, and context — not a demographic variant of an existing persona.
rule.rationale: Teams cannot remember more than 5 personas. Too many personas dilutes focus; team members fall back to designing for whoever they imagined first.

section.name: creation-process

rule.statement: Conduct research first (interviews primary; surveys, analytics, support tickets, sales input as supplements), then identify behavioral clusters, then create personas, then validate with stakeholders.
rule.rationale: Personas created without research reinforce team assumptions. The research-to-clustering-to-artifact sequence ensures claims trace back to evidence.

section.p: Step 1: gather data from multiple sources. Step 2: look for clusters sharing similar goals, behaviors, pain points, and context. Step 3: draft personas with evidence citations. Step 4: validate with stakeholders and team members. Step 5: share across the organization and reference in design decisions.

rule.statement: Every Goal, Frustration, and Behavior field must cite at least one evidence ID from source data. Mark inferences explicitly as [INFERENCE]. Do not invent quotes — use verbatim quotes from identified participants (anonymized IDs).
rule.rationale: Evidence-free inferences are indistinguishable from opinions. Evidence IDs allow audits and rebuild trust when stakeholders challenge findings.

rule.statement: Cap evidence-free inferences at 20% per persona. Refresh research-based personas after every product pivot or new market entry; refresh proto-personas after the first 5 user interviews close.
rule.rationale: Personas decay as products and markets evolve. A stale persona actively misleads: teams make confident decisions based on a user type that no longer exists.

rule.statement: In every design review, frame feature questions against specific primary personas by name: "Would Sarah understand this view?" and "How does this help Sarah's goal of quick insights?"
rule.rationale: Abstract user references ("the user") invite everyone to project their own model. Named persona references force the conversation onto documented, shared evidence.

rule.statement: Write user stories using the persona name as the subject: "As Sarah, I want to see a performance summary so that I can quickly prepare for meetings."
rule.rationale: Persona-sourced stories are auditable: if the story cannot be traced to a goal or frustration in the persona, it may be a hidden assumption masquerading as a user need.

rule.statement: When prioritizing features, state which persona is served and at what tier: "This feature helps Sarah (primary) but not Tom (secondary). Prioritize for Sarah."
rule.rationale: Without explicit persona mapping, prioritization debates have no resolution mechanism beyond seniority or volume.

rule.statement: Maintain a "non-persona" alongside each primary persona set — an explicit statement of who the product is NOT for. Use it to reject features in reviews.
rule.rationale: Saying no in design reviews requires negative space. A non-persona makes the rejection grounded and depersonalized.

section.name: jtbd-connection
section.p: Personas and Jobs-to-be-Done are complementary, not competing: persona answers WHO is using the product; JTBD answers WHAT job they are hiring it for. Example: Sarah (persona) hires the dashboard (product) to make her look informed in leadership meetings (job). Pairing them enables both empathy and precise value-proposition scoping.

rule.statement: Keep personas as living Markdown in a research repository (not PDFs). Diff history shows when assumptions change. Reference the same persona in user stories, A/B test hypotheses, and onboarding flows.
rule.rationale: PDF personas become posters. Posters do not affect decisions. Version-controlled files allow the team to audit drift and trace which research wave updated which field.

antipattern.description: Nobody uses the personas after creation.
antipattern.reason: Personas built without a plan for integration into design reviews, user stories, and prioritization are posters. Usage only follows if the team commits to referencing them in rituals.

antipattern.description: Personas consist only of demographic data (age, role, location) with no goals, behaviors, or frustrations.
antipattern.reason: Demographics tell you nothing about what to build. Goals and behaviors are the design levers; demographic data is context at best.

antipattern.description: More than 5-6 primary personas exist for a single product.
antipattern.reason: Teams cannot hold more than ~5 personas in working memory. Excess personas cause designers to default to their own mental model because the persona set is too large to use.

antipattern.description: Personas were created once and never updated despite product pivots or new markets.
antipattern.reason: Stale personas mislead. A persona describing a 2021 user in a product that has since expanded to a new geography is worse than no persona — it is confident misinformation.

antipattern.description: Personas created from team assumptions without any user research.
antipattern.reason: Assumption-based personas reinforce existing biases rather than revealing user reality. They feel validated because they look like real personas.

antipattern.description: Quote fields contain synthesized, "voicey" paraphrases invented by the author rather than verbatim participant quotes.
antipattern.reason: Synthetic quotes tank credibility at first audit and are indistinguishable from hallucination. Use verbatim quotes with anonymous participant IDs only.

antipattern.description: LLM generates plausible quotes and demographics without grounding them in source transcripts.
antipattern.reason: LLMs hallucinate fluent, contextually appropriate-sounding quotes. Locking the drafter to evidence-only mode and rejecting outputs with quotes not in the source corpus prevents this.

antipattern.description: Persona names and photos encode demographic stereotypes (always-female-marketer, always-male-CTO).
antipattern.reason: Stereotype-encoded personas bias design decisions and are embarrassing in stakeholder reviews. Audit the persona set for diversity and rotate names and photos per update cycle.

antipattern.description: Agent merges research data from different markets or locales into a single persona without explicit instruction.
antipattern.reason: Cultural assumptions embedded in one-market data (US tech-comfortable manager) do not transfer across markets. Segment by locale first, create personas second.

section.name: agentic-workflow
section.p: LLMs are excellent at clustering qualitative data and drafting persona prose; they are weak at deciding which clusters matter. Use an analysis agent to read tagged interview transcripts and propose 6-10 clusters with evidence; a curation agent (with human review) selects the 3-5 personas to keep; a drafting agent fills the persona template citing source quotes verbatim. Persist evidence links — every claim in the persona should map to a transcript span.

Recommended subagents: interview-clusterer (sonnet; ingests tagged transcripts, returns clusters with goal/behavior/pain themes and evidence quotes); persona-drafter (haiku; fills persona template from a cluster definition + evidence pack); persona-validator (sonnet; reviews a persona for evidence coverage, anti-pattern detection (just-demographics, made-up quotes), and JTBD linkage); proto-persona-author (haiku; produces lightweight proto-personas with explicit "assumption" tags).

Prompt pattern: You are persona-drafter. Inputs: cluster_summary, evidence_quotes_with_source_ids. Produce a persona using the template. Every Goal, Frustration, and Behavior MUST cite at least one evidence_id. Mark inferences with [INFERENCE]. Do NOT invent quotes — pick the most representative quote_id for the Quote field. Output: markdown + JSON {evidence_map: {field -> [evidence_ids]}}.

section.name: cli-tools
section.p: dovetail CLI / API — Tag and cluster interview transcripts (dovetail.com/api). condens API — Research repo with thematic tagging (condens.io). marvinapp (Marvin) — Qualitative analysis with API (heymarvin.com). whisperx — Transcribe interviews locally (github.com/m-bain/whisperX). openai-whisper — OSS transcription (github.com/openai/whisper). airtable-cli / notion-cli — Persona library storage and updates (airtable.com / notion.so). pandoc — Convert persona markdown to slides/PDF for stakeholders (pandoc.org).

section.name: services-and-apps
section.p: Dovetail (SaaS, Yes — REST + Insights API): Industry standard for research repos. Condens (SaaS, Yes — REST): Smaller player, strong tagging. Marvin (SaaS, Yes — REST): AI-assisted theming. Notably (SaaS, Yes — REST): AI-native research repo. UserTesting (SaaS, Yes — REST): Source of recorded sessions. Maze (SaaS, Yes — REST): Survey + tree test data into personas. Airtable / Notion (SaaS, Yes — REST): Lightweight persona library hosting.

section.name: best-practices
section.p: Cap evidence-free inferences at 20 % per persona and tag them explicitly. Anything more, the persona is fiction. Pair every persona with a "non-persona" (someone the product is explicitly NOT for). Saying no in design reviews requires negative space. Keep personas as living markdown in a research repo, not as PDFs. Diff history shows when assumptions change. Use the same persona in user stories, A/B test hypotheses, and onboarding flows. If it never re-appears, you built a poster. Cite real quotes verbatim with attribution to anonymous IDs. Synthesized "voicey" quotes tank credibility on first audit. Refresh research-based personas at least once per major release; refresh proto-personas the moment the first 5 user interviews close.

section.name: ai-agent-gotchas
section.p: LLMs hallucinate plausible quotes and demographics. Lock the drafter to evidence-only mode and reject outputs with quotes not present in the source corpus. Cluster quality depends on tagging consistency. Run an inter-rater check on tags before clustering — agents amplify tag noise. Persona "names + photos" risk encoding stereotypes (always-female-marketer, always-male-CTO). Audit the set for diversity and rotate per-update. Proto-personas drift toward team biases. Force the author agent to surface 3+ assumptions per proto and link each to a planned validation method. Beware the "synthetic users" pitch — generating personas purely from LLM imagination yields fluent fiction with no signal. Real research is required. Never let the agent merge research data from different markets/locales into a single persona without explicit instruction; segment first, persona-fy second. Persona files referenced in prompt context can balloon. Keep a one-paragraph persona summary for prompt injection and link to the full doc by URL/ID.

section.name: references
section.p: Alan Cooper, "About Face" (4th ed.) and "The Inmates Are Running the Asylum". Mulder & Yaar, "The User Is Always Right". Kim Goodwin, "Designing for the Digital Age" (research-based persona method). Nielsen Norman Group — "Personas: Practice and Theory" (nngroup.com/articles/persona/). Interaction Design Foundation — Personas guide. Lean UX (Gothelf & Seiden) for proto-persona practice.

template.purpose: Lightweight proto-persona with explicit assumption fields
LLM prompt for evidence-constrained persona drafting

script.purpose: Verify every persona claim cites an evidence ID; fail if quote lacks source
